name: Bangkok: The Royal Island of Rattanakosin

stops.name: Saranrom Park
stops.shortDescription: A peaceful public park that formerly served as the royal garden of Saranrom Palace. It features Victorian-era bandstands, manicured lawns, and is a favorite local spot for evening aerobics.

stops.name: Wat Pho
stops.shortDescription: Known as the Temple of the Reclining Buddha, it is one of Bangkok's oldest and largest temple complexes. It is also considered the birthplace of traditional Thai massage, which is still taught on-site.

stops.name: Tha Tien Pier
stops.shortDescription: A bustling historic market and ferry pier on the Chao Phraya River. From here, you can watch the river traffic or take a quick cross-river ferry to see Wat Arun.

stops.name: The Grand Palace
stops.shortDescription: The spectacular official residence of the Kings of Siam since 1782. Its dazzling buildings blend traditional Thai architecture with European Renaissance styles.

stops.name: Wat Phra Kaew
stops.shortDescription: Located within the Grand Palace grounds, this is Thailand's most sacred Buddhist temple. It houses the revered Emerald Buddha, meticulously carved from a single block of jade.

stops.name: Bangkok City Pillar Shrine
stops.shortDescription: Constructed when Bangkok was established as the new capital in 1782. Locals come here to make offerings and pray to the guardian spirits of the city for prosperity and protection.

stops.name: Sanam Luang
stops.shortDescription: The vast, open royal field facing the Grand Palace. It has historically been used for royal cremations, traditional kite flying festivals, and massive political rallies.

stops.name: Wat Mahathat
stops.shortDescription: One of the most important centers for Buddhist study and Vipassana meditation in Thailand. It offers a much quieter, contemplative experience compared to the nearby royal temples.

stops.name: Bangkok Amulet Market
stops.shortDescription: A fascinating, dense maze of narrow riverside alleys where dedicated collectors, monks, and locals haggle over thousands of small religious talismans and protective Buddha pendants.

stops.name: Bangkok National Museum
stops.shortDescription: Housed in the former palace of the Vice King, this museum holds the largest and most comprehensive collection of Thai art and historical artifacts in the entire country.

stops.name: Phra Sumen Fort
stops.shortDescription: A striking, white octagonal watchtower sitting proudly by the river. Surrounded by a peaceful park, it is one of only two remaining defensive forts built to protect Rattanakosin Island.

stops.name: Khao San Road
stops.shortDescription: The world-famous, bustling epicenter of backpacker culture. It is a chaotic, vibrant street packed with pad thai vendors, cheap foot massages, neon lights, and lively bars.

stops.name: Democracy Monument
stops.shortDescription: A massive, imposing Art Deco monument dominating a major traffic circle. It was erected to commemorate the 1932 revolution that permanently ended absolute monarchy in Thailand.

stops.name: Wat Saket (The Golden Mount)
stops.shortDescription: A gleaming golden chedi resting atop a massive artificial hill. Climbing its 318 spiraling steps rewards you with ringing prayer bells and panoramic, 360-degree views over the old city.
